exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.

items.sentence: Reports suggest that millions of people still live in (1) ...... (POOR), yet those in the richest nations have become far more (2) ...... (WEALTH) over the past century.
items.explanation: Noun from POOR → live in **poverty**; adjective from WEALTH → more **wealthy**.

items.sentence: Clean water and electricity, which form part of our (3) ...... (DAY) routines, remain unimaginable (4) ...... (LUXURY) for vast numbers of people in the developing world.
items.explanation: Adjective from DAY → **daily** routines; plural noun from LUXURY → unimaginable **luxuries**.

items.sentence: Many people question whether it is (5) ...... (ACCEPT) for governments to reduce foreign (6) ...... (ASSIST) when so many nations still struggle with poverty.
items.explanation: Adjective from ACCEPT → **acceptable**; noun from ASSIST → foreign **assistance**.

items.sentence: Leading (7) ...... (ECONOMY) have argued that greater foreign (8) ...... (INVEST) in emerging markets could help lift millions out of poverty.
items.explanation: Person noun from ECONOMY → leading **economists**; noun from INVEST → foreign **investment**.

items.sentence: Countries with a well-educated workforce tend to become (9) ...... (VALUE) partners in international trade.
items.explanation: Adjective from VALUE → **valuable** partners in trade.

items.sentence: The (10) ...... (REAL) is that closing the gap between rich and poor nations will require long-term commitment from governments around the world.
items.explanation: Noun from REAL → the **reality** is that closing the gap…

items.sentence: My neighbour came **D** into some money when her aunt died.
items.answer: D – into some money when her aunt died.
acceptedAnswers: D – into some money when her aunt died.

items.sentence: Despite her low wages, she manages to put **A** a little money by each month.
items.answer: A – a little money by each month.
acceptedAnswers: A – a little money by each month.
items.explanation: Save regularly → put **by** a little money each month.

items.sentence: Nobody should bank **H** on house prices coming down soon.
items.answer: H – on house prices coming down soon.
acceptedAnswers: H – on house prices coming down soon.
items.explanation: Rely on → bank **on** house prices coming down.

items.sentence: It's hard to imagine how they can get **F** by on such a low salary.
items.answer: F – by on such a low salary.
acceptedAnswers: F – by on such a low salary.
items.explanation: Survive financially → get **by** on a low salary.

items.sentence: Could you possibly make **B** out the cheque to Peardrop Limited, that would be fine.
items.answer: B – out the cheque to Peardrop Limited, that would be fine.
acceptedAnswers: B – out the cheque to Peardrop Limited, that would be fine.
items.explanation: Write a cheque → make **out** the cheque.

items.sentence: Every retailer on the high street is giving **E** away free software with every computer.
items.answer: E – away free software with every computer.
acceptedAnswers: E – away free software with every computer.

items.sentence: He's been saving **C** up to buy a new DVD player.
items.answer: C – up to buy a new DVD player.
acceptedAnswers: C – up to buy a new DVD player.
items.explanation: Accumulate for purchase → saving **up** to buy a DVD player.

items.sentence: I really can't think what we're going to live **G** on now I've lost my job.
items.answer: G – on now I've lost my job.
acceptedAnswers: G – on now I've lost my job.
items.explanation: Survive using money → live **on** now I've lost my job.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
They made me pay a fee to use the hotel gym. **charged**
I ...... using the hotel gym.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive, so the correct answer is "was charged for".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was charged for", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Whose credit card is this? **belong**
Does ...... you?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is belong to, so the correct answer is "this credit card belong to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "this credit card belong to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
There isn't much flour left in the bag. **amount**
There's only ...... flour left in the bag.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is small quantity, so the correct answer is "a small amount of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "a small amount of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
That designer handbag was incredibly expensive to buy! **fortune**
It ...... to buy that designer handbag!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is expensive idiom, so the correct answer is "cost a fortune".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "cost a fortune", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
The customer insisted that the manager apologise to him. **apology**
The customer demanded ...... the manager.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is demand + noun, so the correct answer is "an apology from".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "an apology from", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Having a car means I don't have to walk to work every day. **saves**
Having a car ...... to walk to work every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is save from, so the correct answer is "saves me from having".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Don't pay attention to what your critics say; your work is excellent! **notice**
Take ...... what your critics say; your work is excellent!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ignore idiom, so the correct answer is "no notice of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "no notice of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "no notice of" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
I don't have enough money to buy that jacket right now. **short**
I ...... money to buy that jacket right now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lack money, so the correct answer is "am short of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "am short of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Would you be willing to lend me some money? **borrow**
Could ......?
items.answer: I borrow some money from you
acceptedAnswers: I borrow some money from you

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is polite request, so the correct answer is "I borrow some money from you".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "I borrow some money from you", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentenceBefore: When I spoke to Greg last week, he mentioned that he
items.sentenceAfter: of changing careers, but he seems to have reconsidered.

wrongOptions.B: "is thinking" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When you see a finished past-time expression, reach for the past simple first.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: A finished past time marker (yesterday, last week, ago) requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.
